title: attention-result.v1 — the ranking function's ordered-partition output
description: What the attention ranking function hands back: an ordered partition of candidate work across the five fixed budget categories, under hard cross-category precedence, with each category's share of the bounded budget and the candidates ordered best-first inside it. This is the one result contract the orientation events (boot) and the build's risk read (build-orchestration) consume — they inherit this shape here, at the producer, rather than each re-deriving it. The form is fixed (an ordered partition over the five categories plus the budget split, decision-log D-117); only this serialization is settled here. The result is pure data: it records which inputs were missing in 'degraded_inputs' but never narrates that in prose — surfacing a degraded picture loudly and in plain language is boot's job, not this file's.

schema_version.description: Which version of this result shape this is. It lets a later consumer recognise an older result and adapt safely. Today the only valid value is 1.

as_of.description: The single reference moment the ranking was computed against, echoed back exactly as it was supplied (a UTC moment ending in Z, for example 2026-06-04T00:00:00Z). Recency-dependent ordering is reproducible because this is recorded: the same candidates plus the same as_of always yield this same ordering. The ranking never reads the wall clock on its own — this value is always an input.

as_of_is_wallclock.description: True when 'as_of' fell back to the machine's current clock because the cursor carried no refreshed debt timestamp; false when 'as_of' came from a real recorded source. A consumer must not treat a wall-clock as_of as a 'when the debt count was refreshed' timestamp — it only timestamps this ranking run.

session_condition.description: The session-load reading that drove the budget flex: 'clean' (little or no blocking debt — more room is given to orientation) or 'high_debt' (enough blocking debt that orientation is compressed to make room). Recorded so a consumer inherits WHY the budget split flexed the way it did, rather than re-deriving the condition.

partition.description: The five fixed budget categories, in their locked order of importance, each holding its candidates ordered best-first. The array order IS the cross-category precedence: index 0 outranks index 1, and so on, structurally — a lower-importance category can never overtake a higher one whatever the per-candidate weights. Every category is always present, even when it holds nothing, so a consumer sees the whole five-part frame every time; an empty category means its source was absent or yielded nothing, and that emptiness is recorded, never omitted.

category.description: Which of the five fixed categories this entry is, from the locked vocabulary: blocking_debt (what is blocking the work), in_flight (work already underway), recent_decisions (what was decided recently), structural_neighbors (the parts of the project next to what is being touched), orientation (general bearings).

precedence_rank.description: The hard cross-category importance rank this category carried for this run (1 = surfaced first). Recorded so a consumer can SEE that the ordering was structural — and so the diagnostic 'precedence removed' state (every category at the same rank) is visible in the artifact itself rather than only in the tool's behaviour.

budget_fraction.description: This category's share of the bounded budget for this run, after any flex was applied — a fraction between 0 and 1. This is the applied (post-flex) split the consumer uses directly; it never has to re-apply the flex. The five fractions are meant to sum to one whole.

budget_size.description: How many slots of the bounded budget this category was allotted, present only when the run was given a concrete total budget to apportion. The integer realisation of budget_fraction. Omitted when no total budget was supplied (the caller — boot — owns the event's budget total).

members.description: This category's candidates, ordered best-first by the within-category ranking. Each entry is a lightweight reference (the id a consumer resolves against the substrate, plus its position), not a copy of the candidate's full detail: this contract carries the ORDERING, while the substrate keeps the data. An optional 'signals' block carries the ranking inputs the function already computed, so a consumer that needs them (the build's debt-proximity read) need not recompute or re-resolve.

id.description: The candidate's stable identifier — a knowledge entity id, a tracked-issue reference, or a state pointer key — by which a consumer resolves its full detail.

rank.description: This candidate's 1-based position within its category, best-first. Deterministic; equal-ranked candidates are ordered by id so the result is stable.

signals.description: The within-category ranking inputs the function used for this candidate, surfaced so a consumer need not recompute them. Every field is optional — a candidate carries only the signals its category uses (severity for debt, proximity for structural neighbours, recency for time-ordered kinds).

severity.description: The debt severity that fed ranking and the blocking decision, when this candidate is an open-debt item.

proximity.description: How structurally close this candidate is to the work in hand (higher is closer), when this candidate is a structural neighbour.

recency.description: The candidate's own reference moment used for recency ranking, as a UTC moment ending in Z, or null when it carries none.

degraded_inputs.description: The substrate inputs that were ABSENT for this run, by name and sorted — the substrates the ranking could not consult. Today the ranking does not consult the live debt register (telemetry) — it uses an offline cached count from state and leaves the live read to boot/telemetry — so telemetry appears here on every run; on an outage any of state, knowledge, telemetry, or git can appear. An empty array means every substrate was available. This is the machine-readable hand-off boot reads and surfaces loudly in plain language; the result itself records the absence and narrates nothing.
